title: Bank Of Ireland Mortgage Bank -v- Seery and Anor
text:
APPROVED
REDACTED
AN ARD-CHÚIRT
THE HIGH COURT
[2026] IEHC 80
Record No: 2018/269CA
Record No: 2024/264CA
BETWEEN/
BANK OF IRELAND MORTGAGE BANK
PLAINTIFF
AND
EDMUND SEERY AND ANN SEERY
DEFENDANTS
AND
MARS CAPITAL FINANCE IRELAND DESIGNATED ACTIVITY COMPANY
APPLICANT/MOVING PARTY
JUDGMENT of Mr. Justice Conleth Bradley delivered on the 13th day of February 2026

INTRODUCTION
1. This judgment comprises (i) an appeal by the first named defendant (by Notice of Appeal dated
13th July 2018) against an order for possession made by the Circuit Court on 6th July 2018 of a
property at Cloneyheigue, Kilbeggan, County Westmeath, comprised in Folio 21560F of the
Register of Freeholders, County Westmeath (“the property”, “mortgaged property” or “subject
property”) and an (ii) an appeal, also by the first named defendant, (by Notice of Appeal dated
15th November 2024) against an order made by the Circuit Court on 12th November 2024
substituting Mars Capital Finance Ireland DAC as the plaintiff for Bank of Ireland Mortgage
Bank.
2. The subject property is the principal private residence and family home of Mr. Edmund Seery
and Mrs. Ann Seery (“the defendants”) who are estranged. The applicant and moving party is
Mars Capital Finance Ireland Designated Activity Company (“Mars CFI DAC”).
3. In Pepper Finance Corporation (Ireland) DAC v O’Reilly [2025] IECA 140 (Costello P.,
Whelan J. and Binchy J.) Costello P., at para. 22 of her judgment, described the High Court’s
jurisdiction in similar applications, as follows:
“22. An appeal from the Circuit Court to the High Court is different to an appeal from the
High Court to the Court of Appeal or the Supreme Court. The hearing is de novo. The High
Court considers the matters afresh and makes its decision based on the evidence adduced
before the High Court. Evidence before the Circuit Court is not evidence before the High
Court and cannot form the basis of the judgment of the High Court. It is not concerned with
whether the Circuit Court erred in its decision. When the Circuit appeal is heard, the High
2

Court must determine whether, at the time of the application before it, the party who seeks
to be substituted as the plaintiff is entitled to judgment against the defendant: it is not
concerned with whether the party who was plaintiff in the Circuit Court was entitled to
judgment against the defendant. To that extent, it is an analogous stage in the proceedings
as pertained in IBRC v Comer [2014] IEHC 671 (per Kelly J. (as he then was)) and IBRC
v Morrissey i.e. pre-trial.”
4. By virtue, therefore, of s. 37(2) and s. 38(2) of the Courts of Justice Act 1936, such appeals are
“heard by way of rehearing of the action or matter in which the judgment or order the subject
of such appeal was given”, i.e., a de novo hearing or a rehearing of the matters.
5. In these applications, therefore, adopting the observations of Costello P. at paras 22 and 23 of
the judgment of the Court of Appeal in Pepper Finance Corporation (Ireland) DAC v O’Reilly
[2025] IECA 140, I must consider whether the intended assignee, Mars CFI DAC, is, at the
date of the hearing of these appeals (on 23rd October 2025 and 31st October 2025), entitled to
an order for possession. The hearing is de novo and Mars CFI DAC is required to prove its
claimed entitlement to an order for possession and in that regard, that it is the assignee of the
Loan as secured by the Mortgage and Charge. It must prove the case in full, not simply that it
is the beneficiary of an order of the Circuit Court. If it succeeds, a new order for possession
will be granted, and it is that order, rather than the order of the Circuit Court, which is subject
to enforcement.
6. In Pepper Finance Corporation (Ireland) DAC v O’Reilly [2025] IECA 140, KBC Bank Ireland
plc had transferred its interests to KBC Bank NV, pursuant to S.I. 447/2023, and KBC Bank
NV had allegedly assigned the interest in the loan and security underlying the loan to Pepper
3

Finance Corporation Finance (Ireland) DAC (“Pepper”, as the assignee). An ex parte
application had been made to the High Court, pursuant to O. 17, r. 4 of the Rules of the Superior
Courts 1986 (“the RSC 1986”) to substitute the assignee (Pepper) as plaintiff in the proceedings
and the High Court (Ferriter J.) ordered that the proceedings be amended by substituting Pepper
for KBC Bank Ireland plc as the plaintiff in the title of those proceedings and the order
contained a proviso that the defendant be notified of certain matters in writing. Whilst different
parties are involved in these appeals, similar, though not identical, sequential issues arise in the
applications before me, as arose in Pepper Finance Corporation (Ireland) DAC v O’Reilly.
7. In addition to the written submissions furnished, many of the points raised by the first named
defendant were set out in his Affidavits.
8. Whilst these appeals are de novo hearings, it is apposite to set out the background and
chronology which is common to each of the applications, and which are determined in this
single composite judgment.
9. At the hearing before me, the following core documentation was referred to in relation to the
appeal against the possession order of 6th July 2018: Civil Bill for Possession dated 8th June
2010; Grounding Affidavit of Raymond Lawless dated 18th May 2010 and the documents
exhibited thereto; defendants’ Entry of Appearance dated 14th June 2010; first defendant’s
Notice of Change of Solicitor dated 11th February 2011; second defendant’s Entry of
Appearance dated 9th March 2011; Affidavit of Lorcan Brophy dated 10th June 2011 and the
documents exhibited thereto; Affidavit of Fiona Cassidy dated 17th February 2012 and the
documents exhibited thereto; an Order of the Circuit Court dated 21st February 2012; plaintiff’s
Notice of Change of Name and Address of Solicitor dated 14th February 2012; plaintiff’s Notice
of Intention to Proceed dated 23rd February 2015; plaintiff’s Notice of Re-Entry dated 24th
4

March 2015; Verifying Affidavit of John Hughes dated 26th November 2014; 2nd Affidavit of
Fiona Cassidy dated 31st November 2014 and the documents exhibited thereto; Affidavit of
Brendan Moriarty dated 3rd November 2015 and the documents exhibited thereto; Order of the
Circuit Court dated 9th November 2015; Supplemental Affidavit of Ruth Tracey dated 11th
January 2016 and the documents exhibited thereto; 1st Affidavit of Edmund Seery dated 15th
January 2016 and the documents exhibited thereto; Affidavit of Service of Una Carter dated
18th January 2016 and the documents exhibited thereto; Order of the Circuit Court dated 20th
January 2016; 2nd Affidavit of Brendan Moriarty dated 9th February 2016 and the documents
exhibited thereto; 1st defendant’s Notice of Motion to cross-examine dated 7th March 2016; 2nd
Affidavit of Edmund Seery dated 7th March 2016; 3rd Affidavit of Edmund Seery dated 11th
April 2016 and the documents exhibited thereto; Order of the Circuit Court dated 21st April
2016; plaintiff’s Notice of Motion to transfer dated 14th November 2016; 3rd Affidavit of
Brendan Moriarty dated 14th November 2016 and the documents exhibited thereto; Affidavit
of Service of Karen Molloy dated 21st November 2016 and the documents exhibited thereto;
4th Affidavit of Brendan Moriarty dated 22nd November 2016; Affidavit of Anne Durkin dated
8th February 2018 and the documents exhibited thereto; Affidavit of Service of Bernadette
Rafferty dated 15th March 2018 and the documents exhibited thereto; Affidavit of Service of
Francis Craven dated 15th March 2018 and the documents exhibited thereto; 1st defendant’s
Notice of Motion to refer to the Courts of Justice of the European Union dated 12th March
2018; 4th Affidavit of Edmund Seery dated 12th March 2018 and the documents exhibited
thereto; 1st defendant’s Notice to cross-examine dated 12th March 2018; various Orders of the
Circuit Court dated 20th March 2018; 5th Affidavit of Brendan Moriarty dated 31st May 2018
and the documents exhibited thereto; 1st defendant’s re-issued Notices to cross-examine dated
19th June 2018; 1st defendant’s re-issued Notice of Motion to refer to the Courts of Justice of
the European Union dated 26th June 2018; 5th Affidavit of Edmund Seery dated 26th June 2018;
5

1st defendant’s Notice of Motion to access the DAR dated 26th June 2018; 6th Affidavit of
Edmund Seery dated 26th June 2018; 7th Supplemental Affidavit of Edmund Seery dated 26th
June 2018 and the documents exhibited thereto; Supplemental Affidavit of Helen Dorris dated
3rd July 2018 and the documents exhibited thereto; Affidavit of Service of Dermot Gallagher
dated 3rd July 2018 and the documents exhibited thereto; 8th Supplemental Affidavit of Edmund
Seery dated 4th July 2018; various Orders of the Circuit Court dated 6th July 2018; Notice of
Appeal dated 13th July 2018; and 6th Affidavit of Brendan Moriarty dated 29th April 2019 and
the exhibited attached thereto.
10. The following core documentation was referred to in relation to the appeal against the
substitution order dated 12th November 2024: Notice of Motion dated 27th June 2024;
Grounding Affidavit of Stephen McKeever dated 26th June 2024 and the documents exhibited
thereto; Replying Affidavit of Edmund Seery dated 19th September 2024 and the documents
exhibited thereto; Order of the Circuit Court dated 26th September 2024; Affidavit of Service
of Joe Hastings dated 20th August 2024; Supplemental Affidavit of Stephen McKeever dated
6th November 2024 and the documents exhibited thereto; Witness Summons dated 5th
November 2024; Affidavit of Service of Aimee Scott dated 8th November 2024; Affidavit of
Ann Seery dated 11th November and the documents exhibited thereto; Order of the Circuit
Court dated 12th November 2024; Notice of Appeal dated 15th November 2024; 2nd Affidavit
of Edmund Seery dated 13th January 2025 and the documents exhibited thereto; Notice of
Motion dated 27th March 2025; Order of the High Court dated 24th March 2025; Ex Parte
Motion Docket dated 10th February 2025; Statement of Grounds dated 10th February 2025;
Verifying Affidavit of Edmund Seery dated 10th February 2025 and the documents exhibited
thereto; Submissions of Edmund Seery in respect of an application for judicial review; Notice
of Motion to come off record dated 31st March 2025; Grounding Affidavit of Orla Connolly
6

dated 31st March 2025 and the documents exhibited thereto; Supplemental Affidavit of Eoin
Pentony dated 29th October 2025; and Outline Submissions on Behalf of Edmund Seery.
Loan Facility
11. A Loan Facility was advanced to the defendants by Bank of Ireland Mortgage Bank (“the
Bank”), the then Lender, on 3rd October 2007 in the sum of €483,000 in respect of the purchase
of the property and the period of the agreement was for 30 years. The total amount repayable
was €953,438.40 with the annual percentage rate of charge at 5.3% and the cost of the credit
or loan being €470,438.40.
Mortgage and Charge
12. The Deed of Mortgage and Charge, whereby the defendants secured this loan facility against
the property, giving the lender and its assignees rights to the property in the event of the
defendants defaulting, was executed by the Bank and the defendants on 5th March 2008.
13. In Part B of this instrument under the subheading “Definitions”, the ‘all sums nature’ of the
mortgage security is reflected in its terms.
14. The terms “collateralise” is defined as meaning “to include in a mortgage pool and execute or
otherwise create an Encumbrance over the legal or equitable benefit of this Mortgage
including the security hereby created on the Mortgaged Property, the Mortgagee’s rights,
estates, title and interests under this Mortgage and the Mortgagee’s rights and interests
(whether legal or equitable) under any secured loan for the purpose of securing all and any
indebtedness, obligations and liabilities whatsoever or howsoever arising, at any time, both
present and future, actual or contingent and whether as principal or surety of the Mortgagee,
7

or of any subsidiary, parent or associated company of the Mortgagee Bank, or (at the discretion
of the Mortgagee) of any other person, in favour of any person as part of a collateralisation
scheme and cognate words have a corresponding meaning.”
15. Under the sub-heading at para. 8 dealing with “The Security”, para. 8.07(a) of the Deed of
Mortgage dated 5th March 2008 (the Home Loan Consent Mortgage and Charge, Deed of
Confirmation and Release dated 5th March 2008) provides that “the Mortgagee may at any time
and from time to time transfer or enter into contractual arrangements concerning all or part
of the legal or equitable benefit of this Mortgage including the security hereby created on the
Mortgaged Property, the Mortgagee’s estates, rights, title and interests in the Mortgage and
the Mortgagee’s rights and interests (whether legal or equitable under, or legal or equitable
benefit of, any secured loan to any person (including, without prejudice to the generality of the
foregoing, any subsidiary, parent or associated company of the Mortgagee) on such terms as
the Mortgagee may think fit, without notice to the Mortgagor or any other person, whereupon
all powers and discretions of the Mortgagee and all other estates, rights, title and interests
shall, for so long and to the extent provided for in or on such transfer, be held and be exercisable
by the person to whom the Mortgage is transferred”.
16. Under the sub-heading at para. 10 dealing with “Notices”, the Deed of Mortgage dated 5th
March 2008 provides at para. 10.01 that “Any notice or demand made by the Mortgagee under
this Mortgage or by virtue of any statute shall (without prejudice to any other effective mode
of making the same) be deemed to have been properly served if served on the Mortgagor
personally or sent by pre-paid ordinary post to the Mortgagor at the Mortgaged Property or
at his usual or last known place of abode or business or if the Mortgagor is a body corporate
at its registered office or any of its principal places of business, and any such notice sent by
8

pre-paid ordinary post shall be deemed to be received the day following the date of such
posting.”
17. At the time of entering upon the Mortgage and Charge, the defendants were legally represented
(also referred to in correspondence from the Bank dated 16th March 2016 in relation to the
second named defendant’s purported notification of cancellation of the mortgage agreement).
18. Whilst the first named defendant, in opposing the substitution application, submits that the
“goodbye” and “hello” correspondence (referred to later in this judgment) were addressed to
his wife’s address, and that he did not receive them (as they have been estranged from in or
around 2018), and that both the Bank and Mars CFI DAC were on notice of his last address,
para. 10 of the Deed of Mortgage and Charge dealing with “Notices” (referred to above) makes
clear that there was a contractual agreement whereby the defendants agreed that any notice
required to be served could be served at the principal residence. It was a matter for the first
named defendant to ensure that any such notices sent to the Mortgaged Property were collected
by him.
19. Further, in the Affidavit of Orla Connolly, Solicitor of the Law Centre Athlone, sworn on 31st
March 2025, the deponent states that the first named defendant was granted legal aid on 29th
April 2019 and that on 21st February 2022, the Board of the Law Centre intended to terminate
his Legal Aid Certificate for lack of engagement. Ms. Connolly explains that the Law Centre
should have applied to come off record in April-May 2022 when it was clear that the first
named defendant did not intend to review or appeal the termination of his Legal Aid Certificate.
An order coming off record was subsequently made. The first named defendant states that it
was this application to come off record by the Law Centre which triggered him serving his
9

notice of intention to proceed or application to re-enter the appeal and not the fact of the
substitution application being issued.
Defendants’ default in repayments
20. Mr. Lawless is the Structured Finance Manager of the Bank and in his Affidavit sworn on 18th
May 2010 he has exhibited the proofs and instruments, including inter alia true copies of the
Loan Facility, Mortgage and Charge, and the following correspondence and other
documentation referred to, which is required for a claim of possession of the property.
21. By letter dated 11th March 2009 from Bank of Ireland Mortgages, exhibited by Mr. Lawless,
the defendants were written to and informed that despite previous requests and reminders, they
had failed to meet the instalment repayments due on their home loan. The letter stated that the
Bank was no longer prepared to accept this situation and called upon the defendants to repay
the entire debt within 10 days of the letter. The letter inter alia stated that in default of receipt
of such payment together with the interest due, proceedings for recovery of the mortgaged
property would be instituted against the defendants without further notice. The loan was
therefore ‘called-in’ on 11th March 2009.
Demand for possession of the property
22. By letters dated 16th April 2009 sent by solicitors for the Bank to both defendants, reference
was made to the letter dated 11th March 2009 demanding full repayment of the total amount
due on foot of the mortgage and the defendants’ failure and refusal to discharge the said sums.
The letter demanded possession of the property inter alia as follows:
10

“In these circumstances therefore TAKE NOTICE that our client NOW REQUIRES
POSSESSION of the property referred to in the heading of this letter for the purpose
of a sale thereof by them as Mortgagees in possession. We therefore demand, on their
behalf, that you quit and deliver up clear vacant possession of the said property to them
by clearing the property and delivering the keys thereof to our Clients at New Century
House, IFSC, Mayor Street Lower, Dublin 1.
FURTHER TAKE NOTICE that if you do not so deliver up possession within seven
days of the date of this letter our Clients will, without further notice to you, take all
necessary steps to protect and realise their security including, if necessary, enforcing
their rights in relation to possession of the property and including, if deemed
appropriate, the issue of Court proceedings against you seeking an Order compelling
you to deliver possession. Costs incurred in connection with any such steps or
proceedings, which may be substantial, will be your responsibility in accordance with
the terms of your Mortgage Contract.
We would respectfully suggest that you should treat this matter as one of the utmost
urgency and that you should immediately take legal advice as to your position”.
Possession proceedings
23. A Civil Bill for Possession and Special Indorsement of Claim then issued on 8th June 2010 and
is grounded on the Affidavits of Raymond Lawless sworn on 18th May 2010 and matters are
further addressed in the Affidavits of Lorcan Brophy, Senior Credit Manager of the Bank,
sworn on 10th June 2011 and the Affidavit of Fiona Cassidy, Manager in the Arrears Support
Unit of the Bank, sworn on 17th February 2012.
11

24. The evidence is that the Bank (and later, Mars CFI DAC), at all times kept the defendants fully
aware of their level of indebtedness after proceedings issued on 8th June 2010.
25. In his Affidavit sworn on 10th June 2011, Mr. Brophy states at that date a total sum of
€528,731.36 was outstanding on the defendants’ mortgage loan account (including arrears of
€69,632.61 and all interest to that date but excluding legal costs).
26. A Statement of Account for the period from 1st January 2010 to 1st February 2012 exhibited by
Ms. Cassidy shows that arrears in payments due had increased from €36,873.37 to €83,901.15
and the total sum outstanding was €537,405.72 as of 12th February 2012.
27. Ms. Cassidy swore a second Affidavit which was filed on 25th November 2014 where she avers
that at all times, including that in the lead-up to and the prosecution of these proceedings, the
Bank had operated in compliance with the Code of Conduct for Mortgage Lenders (“the Code”)
and states that when the proceedings were commenced on 8th June 2010, the Bank was fully
entitled, under provision 4(d) of the February 2010 edition of the Code (which was then
applicable), to issue these proceedings at that time in circumstances where the defendants’ loan
account had by then, been in arrears for a period well in excess of 12 months and at the time of
the swearing of that Affidavit, the last payment to the account was €400, made on 26th October
2012.
28. The Affidavit detailed other aspects of the Bank’s compliance with the Code and exhibits
correspondence with the defendants, which confirms that the Bank had maintained contact with
the defendants, through their nominated representatives, and states that at no time since the
12

issue of the proceedings has any alternative repayment arrangement been deemed feasible,
agreed or operating.
29. This Affidavit also grounded the Bank’s application to re-enter the proceedings in November
2014 and at paragraph 8 of her Affidavit filed on 25th November 2014, Ms. Cassidy avers that
the Bank is “mindful of the difficulties in which the Defendants find themselves and has held
back from pressing these proceedings with expedition. However, as appears from the
foregoing, the Defendants’ circumstances are such that they cannot sustain or service, let alone
repay, the level of debt associated with the Property and therefore the protraction of the
proceedings is merely causing their indebtedness to increase further by the accrual and
charging of further interest” and sought the orders in terms of the Civil Bill for Possession and
the Notice of Re-Entry.
30. A Notice of Intention to Proceed dated 23rd February 2015 was issued by the Bank and in that
regard, paragraph (3) of the Affidavit of John Hughes, bank official, dated 26th November 2014
confirms that the details of the defendants’ liabilities to the Bank on their loan were, at all
material times, maintained by the Bank in an electronic format on its computerised Mortgage
Accounting System (“MAS”) which constituted the “Bankers Book” for the purposes of the
Banker’s Books Evidence Acts 1879 to 1959 (as amended).
31. In his Affidavit dated 3rd November 2015, Brendan Moriarty, on behalf of the Bank, refers to
and exhibits an original certificate from the Valuation Office dated 2nd November 2015
confirming the rateable valuation of the property as €37.95 and further exhibits an update of
the defendants’ loan account.
13

32. The Affidavit of Ruth Tracey, Legal Case Manager in the Arrears Support Unit of the Bank
confirmed that at that date, 11th January 2016, the defendants’ indebtedness was €571,084.95
(and the mortgage arrears were €165,629.38).
33. The supplemental Affidavit of Helen Dorris, Legal Case Manager in the Arrears Support Unit
of the Bank, sworn on 3rd July 2018, stated that at that time, the last payment made by the
defendants was the payment of €400 made six years previously on 26th October 2012 and that
the balance showing as due on the defendants’ called-up Loan account as of 27th June 2018 was
€589,263.66 (which at that date was €100,000 more that they originally borrowed in 2007).
Arrears on 31st October 2024
34. As of 31st October 2024, the principal sum outstanding was €378,809.26 (with arrears of
€359,189) giving a total of €666,369.78.
Appeal by Mr. Seery
35. In these applications, Mr. Seery, the first named defendant, is the sole appellant and is a litigant
in person.
36. He appealed (by Notice of Appeal dated 13th July 2018) against an Order for possession in
relation to the subject property made in the Circuit Court on 6th July 2018. (No application to
adduce new evidence has been made by, or on behalf of, the parties).
37. Mrs. Ann Seery, the second named defendant, has taken no part in the proceedings and whilst
she has sworn two affidavits, she has never denied the debt. In 2018, Mrs. Seery sought to
14

claim that the agreement should be cancelled. It was dealt with by the Bank at the time and the
question of cancellation generally is addressed later in this judgment.
38. After the making of the Circuit Court Order on 6th July 2018, the Covid-19 pandemic occurred
which delayed matters for a period. Additionally, it is perhaps a fair observation to say that the
appeal against the order for possession has not been processed with any alacrity by any of the
parties.
39. In terms of the issues raised by the first named defendant, these are set out in the submissions
furnished during the hearing of these applications, and in his various Affidavits, including that
sworn on 11th April 2016, which included his contentions in relation to the jurisdiction of the
court, cancellation of the contract (namely the original loan agreement of 3rd October 2007),
conditions precedent, alleged fraud, alleged unfair terms and conditions in consumer contracts,
original documentation, cross examination of deponents, alleged manipulation of interest rates
and in his conclusion the first named defendant summarises the above points.
Substitution application
40. By Notice of Motion dated 27th June 2024, an application has been brought on behalf of Mars
CFI DAC seeking the following orders: (1) an order pursuant to O. 22, r. 4 of the Circuit Court
Rules, as amended (“the CCR”) substituting the applicant (and moving party) – Mars CFI DAC
– as the plaintiff in the proceedings and directing that the within proceedings shall be carried
on between Mars Finance (Ireland) Designated Activity Company in place of Bank of Ireland
Mortgage Bank, as the plaintiff and the defendants; (2) an order pursuant to O. 36, r. 10 of the
CCR granting the applicant (Mars CFI DAC) leave to issue execution in respect of the Order
for Possession made by the Circuit Court on 6th July 2018; (3) A further order dispensing with
15

service on the defendants of the Circuit Court Order substituting Mars CFI DAC as the plaintiff
is also sought and such other consequential orders as the court may direct and costs. This
application is grounded on behalf of the applicant and moving party – Mars CFI DAC– by
Stephen McKeever, Manager of Mars CFI DAC in an Affidavit which was sworn on 26th June
2024.
41. In opposing the substitution application, the first named defendant takes issue with Mr.
McKeever being an employee of another entity within the Mars Group. Further, he takes issue
with Mr. McKeever’s means of knowledge and states that he is not employed by Mars CFI
DAC but rather Mars Capital Management Ireland DAC and he also claims an alleged breach
of data as against the Bank where he says they released data to contractually unrelated parties
insofar as Mr. McKeever is concerned.
42. The first named defendant submits that because he has pleaded a counter-claim (and relied on
his third Affidavit sworn on 11th April 2016) which includes an alleged plea of fraud, the
appropriate order, having regard to the judgment of the Court of Appeal in Pepper Finance
Corporation (Ireland) DAC v O’Reilly [2025] IECA 140 is to add Mars CFI DAC to the
proceedings rather than substitute it for the Bank.
43. Further, Mr. Seery contends that he did not have an adequate opportunity to reply to the second
Affidavit of Stephen McKeever when the matter was before the Circuit Court and that it was
served on him two working days before the trial before Her Honour Judge Fergus.
16

44. He submits that the “goodbye” and “hello” correspondence was addressed to his wife’s address,
that he did not receive them, and he submits that both the Bank and Mars CFI DAC were on
notice of his last address.
45. He submits that the sale deed was not exhibited.
46. He contends that the notice was dated 3rd October 2023 of a transfer which was dated 2nd
October 2023, i.e., subsequent to the date of the alleged transfer, which the first named
defendant submits is contrary to s. 28(6) of the Supreme Court of Judicature Act (Ireland) 1887.
47. He argues that he should have been allowed to cross examine the deponents for the Bank and
Mars CFI DAC and refers to the witness summons served and the DAR transcript of the
substitution application heard before the Circuit Court.
DISCUSSION AND DECISION
48. In addressing the substitution application, first, I do not consider, for the following reasons,
that there is any substance in the first named defendant’s complaints in relation to the
substitution application.
49. In his Affidavit sworn on 26th June 2024, grounding the substitution application, Mr. McKeever
confirms, at para. 1 that he is a manager for Mars Capital Finance Ireland DAC (i.e., Mars CFI
DAC – the applicant for substitution), and he is employed by Mars Capital Management Ireland
DAC and therefore simply holds separate capacities. He confirms his means of knowledge as
a manager of the applicant Mars CFI DAC and he has the capacity to swear the Affidavit as
17

manager. He confirms that Mars CFI DAC is the regulated entity and holds legal title to the
loans it services. He confirms that he makes the grounding Affidavit sworn on 26th June 2024
on behalf of Mars CFI DAC with its authority from facts within his own knowledge and from
a diligent perusal of its books and records in relation to the defendants and the accounts of the
defendants.
50. At para. 2 of the Affidavit sworn on 26th June 2024, Mr. McKeever gives his means of
knowledge and states the books and records reviewed and perused by him in preparation of the
Affidavit and exhibited were prepared in the ordinary course of business and constitute
business records within the meaning of the Civil Law and Criminal Law (Miscellaneous
Provisions) Act 2000.
51. Further, Mr. McKeever averred at para. 6 of his Affidavit sworn on 26th June 2024 that on 2nd
October 2023, Bank of Ireland Mortgage Bank as seller and the applicant (Mars CFI DAC) as
buyer, executed a Deed of Transfer whereby Bank of Ireland Mortgage Bank transferred all its
rights, legal rights, title, interest, estate, and entitlement, past and present, in and under the
Underlying Loans, the Mortgage Assets, and the Finance Documents which included the
facility and the security which are the subject of these proceedings to the applicant and moving
party (Mars CFI DAC) and he exhibits the Transfer Deed dated 2nd October 2023, which while
redacted, includes the property at the following entry “F_XXXX C_XXXX B_XXXX
Cloneyheigue Kilbeggan Co. Westmeath” and makes further reference to each of the defendants
in the context of the mortgage and loans over the property. Mr. McKeever averred at para. 9
and exhibits the Land Registry Form 56 Transfer of Charge (rules 52 and 105) which concerned
the charge over the property, the subject of these proceedings, by which the Bank transferred
18

all its legal rights, title, interest, estate and entitlement, past and present, in the said charge to
the applicant and moving party, Mars CFI DAC.
52. Mr. McKeever avers at paragraph 10 of his Affidavit sworn on 26th June 2024 that the Form 56
was lodged for registration with the Property Registration Authority which was completed and
states that the applicant and moving party, Mars CFI DAC, is now the registered owner of the
said charge. He says and believes that the registration of the applicant, Mars CFI DAC, as
owner of the relevant burden on the Folio is conclusive by reason s. 31 of the Registration of
Title Act 1964 and he exhibits an updated copy of the Folio 21560F which at Entry No. 1
describes “A plot of ground being part of the Townland of CLONYHAGUE and barony of
MOYCASHEL shown as Plan(s) A738W, edged RED on the Registry Map (OS MAP Ref(s)
32R/12, 32/16)”, at Entry No. 2 refers to “15-MAY-2008 D2008NL030462B EDMUND SEERY
of Cloneyheigue, Kilbeggan, County Westmeath and ANN SEERY of Cloneyheigue, Kilbeggan,
County Westmeath are full owners”, at Entry No. 4 refers to “04-APR-2008 D2008NL019972E
Charge for present and future advances repayable with interest. BANK OF IRELAND
MORTGAGE BANK is owner of this charge. Note: The ownership of this charge has been
transferred. See Entry No.7”, and at Entry No. 7 refers to “23-NOV-2023 D2023LR179965C
MARS CAPITAL FINANCE IRELAND DESIGNATED ACTIVITY COMPANY is the owner of
the charge registered at Enry No.4.”
53. The “goodbye letters” dated 3rd October 2023 from the Bank to the defendants are referred to
by Mr. McKeever at para. 11. This correspondence inter alia notified the defendants of the date
of transfer and that the amounts owing to it in respect of the facility had been assigned and sold
to the applicant and moving party, Mars CFI DAC. The “goodbye letters” informed the
19

defendants that the sale took effect on 2nd October 2023 and referred to the defendants’
obligations.
54. By letters dated 11th October 2023, the applicant and moving party, Mars CFI DAC, wrote to
the defendants by way of “hello letters” and again stated that the transfer had been completed
on 2nd October 2023 and referred inter alia to the defendants’ obligations under the contract.
55. In opposing the substitution application, the first named defendant states because the “goodbye
letter” was dated 3rd October 2023 of a transfer of assignment which was dated 2nd October
2023, this is contrary to s. 28(6) of the Supreme Court of Judicature Act (Ireland) 1887 because
he contends that the correct interpretation of that section requires advance notice being given
before the assignment is effective.
56. In Promontoria (Oyster) DAC v Lynn [2022] IEHC 99, the High Court (Simons J.) addressed
these provisions, beginning at para. 18 of his judgment, as follows:
“18. Section 28(6) of the Supreme Court of Judicature Act (Ireland) 1877 provides as
follows: “Any absolute assignment, by writing under the hand of the assignor (not
purporting to be by way of charge only), of any debt or other legal chose in action, of
which express notice in writing shall have been given to the debtor trustee or other
person from whom the assignor would have been entitled to receive or claim such debt
or chose in action, shall be and be deemed to have been effectual in law (subject to all
equities which would have been entitled to priority over the right of the assignee if this
Act had not passed,) to pass and transfer the legal right to such debt or chose in action
from the date of such notice, and all legal and other remedies for the same, and the
20

power to give a good discharge for the same, without the concurrence of the assignor:
Provided always, that if the debtor, trustee, or other person liable in respect of such
debt or chose in action shall have had notice that such assignment is disputed by the
assignor or any one claiming under him, or of any other opposing or conflicting claims
to such debt or chose in action, he shall be entitled, if he think fit, to call upon the
several persons making claim thereto to interplead concerning the same, or he may, if
he think fit, pay the same into the High Court of Justice under and in conformity with
the provisions of the Acts for the relief of trustees.””
57. At para. 19 of his judgment, Simons J. stated that s. 28(6) of the 1877 Act provides that “an
absolute assignment of a debt or other legal chose in action is “effectual in law” if certain
requirements are met. In particular, the assignee (i) will be entitled to pursue all legal remedies
without the concurrence of the assignor; and (ii) will have the power to give a good discharge
for the debt or legal chose in action. In practical terms, this means that the assignee can pursue
proceedings in their own name, without having to join the assignor as a party; and that the
debtor may make payments to the assignee safe in the knowledge that such payments go
towards reducing the debt and that the assignor no longer has any interest in the debt.”
58. At para. 20 of his judgment, Simons J. referred to the fact that the nature of the notification
required by s. 28(6) of the 1877 Act was discussed in AIB Mortgage Bank v Thompson [2017]
IEHC 515; [2018] 3 I.R. 172, where the High Court (Baker J.) summarised the principles (at
paragraphs 48 to 50, and paragraph 53 of the reported judgment) as follows:
“The authorities suggest that a court will look to the substance and not the form of a
notice. I consider that in order to be a valid notice under s. 28(6) the debtor must be
21

given express notice in writing of an assignment of his debt to another, that other must
be identified, and the notice must contain sufficient information to enable the debtor to
know with reasonable certainty that the assignment did assign the debt so that he may
without acting at his peril pay the debt to the identified assignee. The absence of a date
is relevant, and this must be so because s. 28(6) expressly provides in its terms that the
date of the notice to the debtor is the effective date of the assignment for the purposes
of the assignment at law. The 1877 Act does not make provision for who is to give the
notice in writing of the assignment […]
While a notice does not have to be sent with the intention of constituting a statutory
notice, a notice must be sufficiently clear as the legislation requires that the notice be
express. This precludes the argument advanced by the plaintiff that it is sufficient that
documents sent to a debtor by implication identify an assignment, and I do not consider
that s. 28(6) leaves open an argument that a notice which impliedly identifies an
assignment can be sufficient, or that a prior general consent performs the statutory
function of a notice.
A notice must be given, it need not be formal, it need not refer to the statute, but it must
be an express notice of an assignment and not merely a claim to the debt by another
party. The existence of a prior assignment ought not to be implied. There is nothing in
the statute to my mind which suggests that the notice must be contained in one document
and for that reason the joinder of documents may be sufficient to constitute a notice of
assignment.”
59. Accordingly, contrary to the contention of the first named defendant, the purpose of the notice
is that it guarantees that the party who is notified of the assignment has the assurance that any
monies paid on foot of the debt will be effectively discharged against the indebtedness or
22

applied to the indebtedness rather than speculating as to whether or not the assignment has
occurred.

Sequencing
67. The first named defendant seeks to raise an issue in relation to the entitlement of Mars CFI
DAC (and its legal advisers) to maintain these applications.
68. As mentioned at the beginning of this judgment, a similar (though not identical) sequence of
events, to that which has occurred here, was the subject of recent decisions of the Superior
Courts, first, the Court of Appeal in Pepper Finance Corporation (Ireland) DAC v O’Reilly
[2025] IECA 140 (Costello P., Whelan J. and Binchy J.) where Whelan J. and Binchy J. agreed
with the judgment of Costello P.; second, arising from the judgment of the Court of Appeal, the
decision of the High Court (Simons J.) in Pepper Finance Corporation (Ireland) DAC v
O’Reilly [2026] IEHC 16).
69. In answering the initial case stated from the High Court, in Pepper Finance Corporation
(Ireland) DAC v O’Reilly [2025] IECA 140, at para. 50, Costello P. held that generally, in
circumstances where there has been an assignment of interest in a registered charge, and the
25

underlying debt subsequent to the making of an order for possession by the Circuit Court, but
prior to the hearing of an appeal to the High Court, it is appropriate to substitute the
transferee/assignee as plaintiff in lieu of the original plaintiff. Costello P. added that there would
be cases where it would be appropriate to retain the assignor as a party to the proceedings
where, for example, there was a counterclaim against the original plaintiff in relation to which
liability could not have been assigned. In such cases, substitution would not be appropriate
because the original party remained a necessary party and there is not an identity between the
interests of the assignor and the assignee.
70. In Pepper Finance Corporation (Ireland) DAC v O’Reilly [2025] IECA 140, the Court of
Appeal (applying Re Worldport Ireland Ltd [2005] IEHC 189) departed from its previous
decision in Irish Bank Resolution Corporation Ltd v Halpin [2014] IECA 3 (where that court
had refused the application to substitute Kenmare in place of IBRC and, had rather, added it as
an additional party). Costello P. noted (at para. 43) that in the ten years since Halpin had been
decided while many assignors had remained a party to the proceedings, in practice they never
participated in the proceedings after the date of the assignment and further observed that “In
practice the assignee conducts the proceedings and, where judgment has been entered, upholds
the decision of the High Court, with one legal team representing both the assignor and the
assignee. The continued presence of the assignor in the proceedings is superfluous and
potentially merely adds an unnecessary layer of costs.”
71. At para. 48 of the judgment in Pepper Finance Corporation (Ireland) DAC v O’Reilly (Court
of Appeal), reference was made to Stapleford Finance Limited v Lavelle [2016] IECA 104
where Costello J. (as she then was), at para. 20 of the judgment of the Court of Appeal, stated
that the legislative intent of the Supreme Court of Judicature Act (Ireland) 1877 would be
26

defeated if the rules of court do not provide for the substitution of the assignee of the chose in
action as plaintiff in the proceedings commenced by the assignor: “The legislative intent was
to dispense with the unnecessary continuance of the assignor in proceedings in which it no
longer had any interest. That is not achieved if an assignor is required to remain a party to
proceedings where it has assigned its interest in the judgment and underlying loan (and
security) post judgment but pending appeal and it is unnecessary to construe the rules in a
manner which requires this outcome.”
72. At paras 50 and 51 of her judgment in Pepper Finance Corporation (Ireland) DAC v O’Reilly,
Costello P. addresses the circumstances where it is appropriate to substitute the
transferee/assignee as plaintiff in lieu of the original plaintiff and explains why the Court of
Appeal declined to follow its earlier decision in IBRC v Halpin:
“50. … In general, in circumstances where there has been an assignment of interest in
a registered charge, and the underlying debt subsequent to the making of an order for
possession by the Circuit Court, but prior to the hearing of an appeal to the High Court,
it is appropriate to substitute the transferee/assignee as plaintiff in lieu of the original
plaintiff. I say in general because there will be cases where it will be appropriate to
retain the assignor as a party to the proceedings, for example if there is a counterclaim
against the original plaintiff, liability for which could not have been assigned. In such
cases substitution will not be appropriate because the original party remains a
necessary party and there is not an identity between the interests of the assignor and
the assignee.
27

51. Applying the decision in Worldport, I would respectfully decline to follow the
decision of this Court in IBRC v Halpin. There was no requirement in Halpin that IBRC
remain a plaintiff and there was no bar to substitution of Kenmare in place of IBRC
merely because the assignment of the plaintiff’s interest occurred between the granting
of the judgments under appeal and the hearing of an appeal. The continuance of the
assignee in the proceedings is not required, save in the circumstances I have identified
above, and usually substitution of the assignee rather than the addition of the assignee
will be the appropriate order.”
73. In the circumstances of the applications before me, I do not consider, for the reasons set out in
this judgment, that it is appropriate to retain the Bank as a plaintiff in these proceedings and
having regard to the claims and allegations made by the first named defendant in his Affidavits.

“Consumer Rights Directive 2011/83/EC, repealing Council Directive 85/577/EEC
and Directive 97/7/EC
35. With regard to the contention that the defendant is entitled to cancel the agreement

Article 3(2)(a) of the Directive provides that it shall not apply to any contract for the

(a) for social services, including social housing, childcare and support of families
and persons permanently or temporarily in need, including long-term care; …
(e) for the creation, acquisition or transfer of immovable property or of rights in
immovable property;
(f) for the construction of new buildings, the substantial conversion of existing
buildings and for rental of accommodation for residential purposes.” (emphasis
added)
Thus, it does not appear to me that these Regulations are applicable.
Fair Procedures and Summary Proceedings
37. It is argued by the defendant that a process whereby a person’s home may be
repossessed in a summary manner does not adequately vindicate his right to a fair
hearing, is a disproportionate interference and would result in homelessness. The
decision in Connors v. UK [2004] ECHR 223 is called in aid. Implicit in the argument
is that where an order for possession is sought in a summary manner the absence of a
plenary hearing violates the rights of the homeowner because of the threat of
homelessness.
36

38. In my view, the decisions relied upon are not authority for such a wide sweeping
proposition. The procedures and legal principles applicable on an application for
summary possession are such that if an arguable ground of defence is raised, then the
matter must be transferred to plenary hearing. The threshold is low. It has been
reiterated on many occasions, in the context of applications for summary orders, that
the constitutional rights of the parties to litigate, prosecute and defend must be
respected. Thus, the court must ensure that where such an application is brought if
there are arguable grounds of defence, the case ought to be transferred to plenary
hearing. The court must be satisfied on an application for summary possession that it
is clear that there is no defence. See the principles outlined in decisions such as
Harrisrange v. Duncan [2003] 4 I.R. 1.
39. This safety mechanism is consistent with the protection of the Constitutional rights
of both parties. Procedurally, Mr. Brennan has been before the County Registrar, the
Circuit Court (on two occasions) and this court. The procedures applied permitted him
to advance every conceivable point of defence that might arise. I am not satisfied that
any procedural unfairness has been established.
40. Further, to adopt the reasoning of McDermott J. in Davis [Permanent TSB Plc
Formerly Irish Life and Permanent Plc v Davis and Davis [2019] IEHC 184], the
proceedings concern inter partes litigation involving an application under s. 62(7) of
the Act of 1964. That Act enjoys a presumption of constitutionality. In accordance with
that provision the registered owner of a charge has the right to seek and obtain an
order for possession if relevant proofs are established. The rights and obligations
sought to be enforced are those enjoyed by and imposed upon the plaintiff and the
defendant pursuant to an agreement freely entered into by them. As McDermott J. also
observed, the court is not permitted to act solely on the basis of sympathetic factors.
37

That it is, may be, or has become apparent with the passage of time, that the monies
advanced in truth exceeded the capacity of the borrower to repay is most unfortunate.
It does not appear to me, however, on the basis of the arguments advanced and in the
circumstances of this case, that this affects the rights of the parties under the loan
agreement and mortgage”.
99. Further, the observations of McDermott J. in Permanent TSB Plc Formerly Irish Life and
Permanent Plc v Davis and Davis [2019] IEHC 184 which were adopted by MacGrath J. in
KBC Bank Ireland PLC v Brennan [2020] IEHC 247, particularly in para. 40 in the quoted
extract above, are applicable to the arguments raised by the first named defendant. I consider
that there is no basis to the first named defendant’s complaints in relation to these matters.
There is, therefore, no basis for the making of a preliminary reference to the CJEU.
Conditions Precedent
100. In the Mortgage Loan Offer Letter dated 3rd October 2007, at Part 3 – Conditions precedent,
para. (a)(i) which is referable to the first defendant refers to “All documentation must be to the
satisfaction of the Lender” and para. (c)(ii) which is referable to conditions precedent applying
to the load provides that “A valuation (including a photograph) of the Property for mortgage
purposes (see Clause 2(c) of Part 5 – The General Conditions) showing the valuation in an
amount of not less than EUR 600,000 and which must be on terms acceptable to the Lender. A
final inspection will be required from properties under construction/undergoing significant
repair. The Lender may raise further conditions after receipt of the valuation. To qualify for the
Lender’s Quickstart 100 product, properties must have at least two bedrooms.”
38

101. The first named defendant seeks to rely on the conditions precedent at paras 34 to 37 of his
Affidavit sworn on 11th April 2016 and makes reference to the decision of the High Court
(Charleton J.) in IRBC v Cambourne Investments Incorporated and Others [2012] IEHC 262,
[2014] 4 I.R. 54. In his fourth Affidavit sworn on 22nd November 2016, Mr. Moriarty states that
the decision of the High Court in Cambourne is in fact an authority which is against the first
named defendant’s proposition and that judgment in that case was ultimately entered for the
plaintiff bank as against all of the defendants with the court confirming that conditions inserted
into a contract exclusively for the benefit of one party can be waived by that party and further
stating that once lent, money was repayable.
102. I do not consider that there is any basis for the first named defendant’s argument in this regard
in response to the application for possession sought by Mars CFI DAC. The conditions apply
to the defendants and not the lender. They date from 3rd October 2007. From in or around
2007/2008 the defendants received and had the benefit of the loan and in acquiring the property,
the defendants executed charges over that property in favour of the lender, and the mortgage
was registered as a charge in favour of the lender over the property. In so much as the first
named defendant seeks to raise conditions precedent in an Affidavit sworn on 11th April 2016,
he does so eight and a half years after the loan was advanced and when he was legally
represented.
Allegation of fraud by the first named defendant
103. In his Affidavit sworn on 16th January 2016, at paragraph 7, the first named defendant alleges
fraud and refers to addressing “the kernel of the matter between myself and the plaintiff [i.e.,
the Bank], namely the fraud perpetrated at the inception of this agreement by agents for the
bank, which I believe vitiated the agreement that the bank are now trying to ground their claim
39

for possession upon, at a later date.” At para. 24 of his Affidavit, the first named defendant
alleges that the Bank did not come before the court with clean hands and the legal
representatives were aware of it.
104. These allegations are addressed initially in the second Affidavit of Brendan Moriarty, solicitor
of Whitney Moore solicitors, sworn on 9th February 2016, on behalf of the Bank. The
allegations are described by Mr. Moriarty as very serious and neither particularised nor
substantiated. He states and believes, having been informed by counsel that when the
proceedings were before the Circuit Court on 20th January 2016, counsel and the Court had
requested the first named defendant to inform the court what “fraud” had allegedly been
perpetrated and having refused to do so, the Circuit Court (Her Honour Judge Flanagan) granted
the first named defendant six weeks to swear, file and serve an Affidavit setting out full and
particular details of the fraud alleged by him and three weeks thereafter for the Bank to reply.
At the time of the swearing of Mr. Moriarty’s Affidavit on 9th February 2016, as the first named
defendant had not at that time delivered an Affidavit, Mr. Moriarty did not propose to address
what he described as “these wholly improper allegations, other than to say that they are rejected
outright.”
105. In relation to those allegations of fraud which were subsequently set out at paras 38 to 48 of
the first named defendant’s Affidavit sworn on 11th April 2016, Mr. Moriarty Solicitor, in his
fourth Affidavit sworn on 22nd November 2016, states that these allegations show no
wrongdoing on the part of the Bank and indicate, rather, that the defendants, at the urging of
their mortgage broker, approached a number of Valuers until they could find one prepared to
provide a sufficiently optimistic valuation on the subject property for them to convey to the
Bank.
40

106. Despite being requested to particularise his allegations, which he is required to do, the first
named defendant has set out no basis for the allegation that the second of three valuations (the
first was for €450,000, the second was for €450,000 and the third was for €560,000) not being
contained in his data access request, allegedly pointed to a named official intentionally
withholding material information from the Bank and its underwriters (the official referred to
by the first named defendant was an employee of the Governor and Company of the Bank of
Ireland).
107. I do not consider that the allegation of fraud made by the first named defendant forms any basis
for a defence or response to the possession application or the substitution application.
Alleged unfair terms and conditions in Consumer Contracts
108. The terms and conditions in the contractual documentation in this case reflect the standard
documents used in such transactions. Further, the defendants were legally advised when they
entered into these arrangements and there is no basis for the first named defendant’s complaints
in this regard.
Alleged manipulation of interest rates
109. The first named defendant at paras 65 to 67 of his Affidavit of 11th April 2016 makes a
generalised allegation against “the main retail banks in Europe” and “Libor base rates” in
seeking to argue uncertainty in relation to what level of interest repayment may be due to the
lender. This allegation has no relevance to the applications for possession and substitution.
41

CONCLUSION
110. For the reasons set out in this judgment, therefore, I do not consider that there is a basis to the
claims alleged and assertions made by the first named defendant which would warrant not
making an order substituting Mars CFI DAC as the plaintiff in these proceedings and granting
Mars CFI DAC possession of the subject property. Further, having regard to the matters raised
by the first named defendant, I do not consider that he has established any defence or any
arguable grounds of defence which would warrant the application for possession being
transferred to plenary hearing.
111. Insofar as any ancillary arguments are being maintained in this respect, I do not consider that
it is necessary that the various deponents in both the possession application and the substitution
application be made available for cross-examination in order to determine these applications.
112. The underlying proceedings seek to recover possession of the subject property. As set out earlier
in this judgment, I am satisfied that Bank of Ireland Mortgage Bank as seller and Mars CFI
DAC as buyer, executed a Deed of Transfer whereby Bank of Ireland Mortgage Bank
transferred all its rights, legal rights, title, interest, estate, and entitlement, past and present, in
and under the Underlying Loans, the Mortgage Assets, and the Finance Documents which
included the facility and the security which are the subject of these proceedings to Mars CFI
DAC and that the Transfer Deed was dated 2nd October 2023.
113. Similar arguments to those raised by the first named defendant were canvassed in AIB plc and
Everyday Finance DAC v Doran [2025] IEHC 515. In that case, the High Court (Owens J.)
reviewed the applicable case law, including the decision of the High Court (Charleton J.) in
42

IRBC v Cambourne Investments Incorporated and Others [2012] IEHC 262, [2014] 4 I.R. 54,
(referred to earlier) and the authorities referred to in that judgment and observed at para. 213
that “Non-satisfaction of a proof which the bank was contractually entitled to insist on as a
pre-condition to permitting draw-down of Thomas Doran’s loans did not necessarily result in
automatic termination of the contracts embodied in the facility letters: see O’Connor v Coady
([2004] IESC 54) [2004] 3 I.R. 271 per Geoghegan J. at p. 278 para. [11]” and, therefore,
non-compliance with preconditions does not automatically terminate a contract.
114. In Lakeland Agri Ltd v Hand [2021] IEHC 13 the High Court (Simons J.) addressed, on the
facts of that case, the question of the application of the subset of the jurisprudence on summary
summons proceedings which addresses the distinction between a cross-claim which amounts
to a defence, and one which is merely a counterclaim and referred to McGrath v O’Driscoll
[2006] IEHC 195; [2007] 1 I.L.R.M. 203, and Moohan v S. & R. Motors (Donegal) Ltd v
Bradley Construction [2007] IEHC 435; [2008] 3 I.R. 650.
115. At para. 18 of his judgment, Simons J. stated that the overall approach to be taken to a case
involving a cross-claim was summarised by Clarke J. (as he then was) in Moohan v S. & R.
Motors (Donegal) Ltd v Bradley Construction [2007] IEHC 435; [2008] 3 I.R. 650, at pp.
656/657, as follows:
“(a) it is firstly necessary to determine whether the defendant has established a defence
as such to the plaintiff’s claim. In order for the asserted cross-claim to amount to a
defence as such, it must arguably give rise to a set off in equity and must, thus, stem
from the same set of circumstances as give rise to the claim but also arise in
43

circumstances where, on the basis of the defendant’s case, it would not be inequitable
to allow the asserted set off;
(b) if and to the extent that a prima facie case for such a set off arises, the defendant
will be taken to have established a defence to the proceedings and should be given
liberty to defend the entire (or an appropriate proportion of) the claim (or have same,
in a case such as that with which I am concerned, referred to arbitration);
(c) if the cross-claim amounts to an independent claim, then judgment should be entered
on the claim but the question of whether execution of such judgment should be stayed
must be determined in the discretion of the court by reference to the principles set out
by Kingsmill Moore J. in Prendergast v. Biddle (Unreported, Supreme Court, 31st July,
1957).”
116. On the facts of that case, Simons J. had further regard to the judgment of Kingsmill Moore J.
in Prendergast v Biddle (Unreported, Supreme Court, 31st July 1957) and held that the nature
of the counterclaim sought to be advanced by the defendant in that case was simply “too
vague”. The Court granted judgment which order did not preclude the defendant from pursuing,
in separate proceedings, any claim for damages arising out of the alleged breach of the haulage
services contract which was at issue in that case, as it amounted to an independent claim. In
AIB plc and Everyday Finance DAC v Doran [2025] IEHC 515, the High Court (Owens J.)
observed at para. 69 that a contract may be treated by a party who is not in breach as terminated
where that breach which is sufficiently serious to justify refusal of further performance. Where
a contract has been substantially performed this is not possible and the remedy of the party not
in breach is confined to damages.
44

117. A counterclaim is entirely independent of the actual claim, which in this case is a possession
application in the circumstances set out in this judgment. In this case, there is no dispute but
that the defendants received the loan in 2008, used the sums that were loaned and had a
contractual obligation to repay those sums but that within a year of taking out the mortgage and
receiving the loan, the defendants had defaulted. The claims now alleged by the first named
defendant in response to the substitution and possession applications are not made out.
118. For a period of approximately 17 years, the defendants have had the benefit of moneys loaned
to them and failed to make the contractual repayments as per the loan agreement. The loan was
called-in on 11th March 2009 when the defendants were written to and informed that despite
previous requests and reminders, they had failed to meet the instalment repayments due on their
home loan. The letter called upon the defendants to repay the entire debt within 10 days of the
letter and stated that in default of receipt of such payment together with the interest due,
proceedings for recovery of the mortgaged property would be instituted against the defendants
without further notice.
119. By letters dated 16th April 2009, sent to both defendants, reference was made to the previous
correspondence dated 11th March 2009 demanding full repayment of the total amount due on
foot of the mortgage and the defendants’ failure and refusal to discharge the said sums. The
letter to the defendants dated 16th April 2009 demanded possession of the subject property.
Thereafter, and at all material times, the defendants were written to and notified of the
increasing level of indebtedness by reference to statements of account.
120. It has been established at the de novo hearing of these applications over two days in October
2025 that Mars CFI DAC, as the applicant and moving party, is the registered owner of the
45

Mortgage and Charge over the property at Cloneyheigue, Kilbeggan, County Westmeath,
comprised in Folio 21560F of the Register of Freeholders, County Westmeath and more
particularly described as including a plot of ground being part of the Townland of
CLONYHAGUE and barony of MOYCASHEL shown as Plan(s) A738W, edged RED on the
Registry Map (OS MAP Ref(s) 32R/12, 32/16) as per the statement of the law set out in Bank
of Ireland Mortgage Bank v Cody [2021] IESC 26, [2021] 2 I.R. 381 (the Supreme Court, Baker
J.), ss. 62, 62(6), 62(7) of the Registration of Title Act 1964, s. 1 of the Land and Conveyancing
Law Reform Act 2013 and the decision of the High Court (Woulfe J.) in Start Mortgages DAC
v Ryan and Ryan [2021] IEHC 719.
121. The Civil Bill for Possession and Special Indorsement of Claim issued on 8th June 2010 is in
compliance with the Rules of the Circuit Court (S.I. 261/2009), including O. 5B of the CCR.
122. The Register of Freeholders for the County of Westmeath confirms that Mars CFI DAC is the
owner of the Mortgage and Charge on the Folio 21560F which at Entry No. (1) described “A
plot of ground being part of the Townland of CLONYHAGUE and barony of MOYCASHEL
shown as Plan(s) A738W, edged RED on the Registry Map (OS MAP Ref(s) 32R/12, 32/16…),
at Entry No.(2) refers to 15-May-2008 D2008NL030462B-EDMUND SEERY of Cloneyheigue,
Kilbeggan, County Westmeath and ANN SEERY of Cloneyheigue, Kilbeggan, County
Westmeath are full owners, at Entry No. (4) refers to 04-APR-2008 D2008NL019972E Charge
for present and future advances repayable with interest. BANK OF IRELAND MORTGAGE
BANK is owner of this charge. Note: The ownership of this charge has been transferred. See
Entry No.7, and at Entry No. (7) refers to 23-NOV-2023 D2023LR179965C MARS CAPITAL
FINANCE IRELAND DESIGNATED ACTIVITY COMPANY is the owner of the charge
registered at Enry No.4.”
46

123. Accordingly, s. 31 of the Registration of Title Act 1964 provides that the Register is conclusive
evidence of any right, privilege, appurtenance or burden appearing on the register.
124. I am, therefore, satisfied that Mars CFI DAC, as the party moving the application for possession
of the subject property, is the registered owner of the subject Mortgage and Charge, that its
right to possession has arisen and has become exercisable and that it is the appropriate plaintiff
who is entitled to maintain these proceedings and is thereby entitled to an order of possession
of the subject property: see the decision of the Court of Appeal in Tanager DAC v Kane [2018]
IECA 352 [2019] 1 I.R. 385, Start Mortgages DAC v Ryan and Ryan [2021] IEHC 719, Bank
of Ireland Mortgage Bank v Cody [2021] IESC 26, [2021] 2 I.R. 381.
125. The defendants took out the initial loan in 2008 and went into default in their repayments in
2009 and the right to possession of the property vested in the owner of the Mortgage and Charge
has arisen and has become exercisable. Extremely modest repayments were made and the
indebtedness increased year on year over a 17-year period and including the period of these
proceedings. The defendants have not in fact disputed that they are unable to address their
indebtedness. As the owner of the loans, Mars CFI DAC are entitled to be substituted for the
Bank and are further entitled to an order of possession of the subject property.
126. I therefore dismiss the first named defendant’s appeals against the order of the Circuit Court
made on 6th July 2018 in Record No: 2018/269CA and the order of the Circuit Court made on
12th November 2024 in Record No: 2024/264CA.
47

property known as Clonyhague (otherwise Cloneyheigue), Kilbeggan, County Westmeath (“the
